content.text: High availability (HA) is the property of a system that remains operational for a defined percentage of time, measured over a year. Unlike fault tolerance (which targets zero downtime), HA systems accept brief interruptions during failover events but automate recovery so that the cumulative downtime stays within the agreed SLA target.

attrs.alt: Multi-AZ HA architecture: three AZs with public/private/data subnets, NAT gateways, ASG, RDS Multi-AZ, and ElastiCache replicas

content.text: Availability is expressed as a percentage of uptime over a rolling year. Each additional '9' dramatically reduces the allowed downtime:
Most well-designed multi-AZ applications on AWS achieve 99.99% or better. AWS's own service SLAs range from 99.9% (some individual services) to 99.99% (EC2, S3, RDS Multi-AZ) to 100% (Route 53).
When a system depends on multiple independent components, the composite availability is the product of each component's availability. This is the most important formula in HA design:
Example: if your application depends on EC2 (99.99%), RDS (99.95%), and ElastiCache (99.9%), the composite availability for the entire stack is:

This means a stack of three individually reliable services can have composite availability of only 99.84% — just above three nines — because every dependency is a potential point of failure. The implication: minimize the number of synchronous dependencies in the critical path.
Parallel (redundant) components multiply availability upward. Serial (dependent) components multiply it downward. The goal of HA design is to make the critical path as parallel as possible.

Multi-AZ deployment is the baseline requirement for any production workload on AWS. Each Availability Zone is an independent data center with its own power, cooling, and network. An AZ failure — which is rare but has happened — takes down every resource in that AZ simultaneously.
Deploy EC2 instances, RDS, ElastiCache, and NAT Gateways in at least 2 AZs (3 is recommended for better load distribution during AZ failure).
Use managed services that handle multi-AZ automatically: ALB, ELB, Route 53, EFS, DynamoDB.
Verify AZ coverage explicitly — some Terraform configurations silently deploy to only one AZ if subnet IDs are not specified carefully.
ALB is itself a highly available service — AWS manages redundant ALB nodes in each AZ you configure. For HA:
— enabled by default on ALB. Distributes traffic evenly across all registered targets regardless of AZ, compensating for unequal instance counts.
— when a target is deregistered (instance termination, scale-in), the ALB waits for existing connections to complete before stopping routing. Set deregistration delay to 30–60 seconds.
— avoid if possible (stickiness breaks HA). If required, use application-managed sessions stored in ElastiCache rather than ALB session cookies.
— use path-based or host-based routing to keep HA independent per microservice.

resource "aws_lb" "web" {
  name               = "prod-alb"
  internal           = false
  load_balancer_type = "application"
  subnets            = aws_subnet.public[*].id  # All 3 AZs
  security_groups    = [aws_security_group.alb.id]

  # Deletion protection prevents accidental teardown
  enable_deletion_protection = true
}

resource "aws_lb_target_group" "web" {
  port                 = 80
  protocol             = "HTTP"
  vpc_id               = aws_vpc.main.id
  deregistration_delay = 30

  health_check {
    path                = "/health"
    interval            = 15
    healthy_threshold   = 2
    unhealthy_threshold = 2
  }
}
Auto Scaling Groups for Compute HA

An Auto Scaling Group (ASG) ensures a minimum number of healthy EC2 instances are always running across AZs. On AZ failure, the ASG detects unhealthy instances and launches replacements in surviving AZs:
(one per AZ) is the HA baseline. If AZ-A fails, the ASG still has two healthy instances in AZ-B and AZ-C. With min_size = 1, the single instance could be in the failed AZ, causing total outage.
Single-AZ RDS is not HA. Any instance termination, AZ failure, or hardware problem causes downtime. Multi-AZ RDS maintains a synchronous standby replica in a second AZ:
Writes are committed to both primary and standby before the application receives acknowledgment — RPO = 0.
On primary failure, RDS promotes the standby and updates the CNAME of the endpoint within 60–120 seconds — the connection string does not change.
Read replicas are separate from Multi-AZ standby. They are asynchronous and can be promoted manually but are not used in the automatic Multi-AZ failover.

# RDS with Multi-AZ + automated backups
resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
  identifier        = "prod-db"
  engine            = "postgres"
  engine_version    = "16.1"
  instance_class    = "db.r6g.large"
  allocated_storage = 200
  storage_type      = "gp3"
  multi_az          = true

  # Backups for point-in-time recovery
  backup_retention_period   = 7     # 7 days
  backup_window             = "02:00-03:00"
  maintenance_window        = "sun:04:00-sun:05:00"
  deletion_protection       = true
  skip_final_snapshot       = false
  final_snapshot_identifier = "prod-db-final"
  db_subnet_group_name      = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
}
Aurora takes Multi-AZ further. The Aurora storage layer writes each transaction to 6 storage nodes across 3 AZs. A write succeeds when 4 of 6 nodes acknowledge:
Tolerates loss of an entire AZ (losing 2 of 6 storage nodes) with zero data loss.
Writer failover to a reader takes under 30 seconds for Aurora MySQL/PostgreSQL.
Aurora Global Database adds cross-region replication with < 1 second lag, supporting disaster recovery.
Aurora Serverless v2 adjusts writer and reader capacity independently with fine-grained ACU (Aurora Capacity Units) scaling.

# Aurora cluster with 3 AZs
resource "aws_rds_cluster" "aurora" {
  cluster_identifier = "prod-aurora"
  engine             = "aurora-postgresql"
  engine_version     = "15.4"
  database_name      = "myapp"
  master_username    = "admin"
  master_password    = var.db_password
  availability_zones = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
  backup_retention_period = 14
  db_subnet_group_name    = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
}

# Writer + 2 readers, one per extra AZ
resource "aws_rds_cluster_instance" "instances" {
  count              = 3
  identifier         = "aurora-${count.index}"
  cluster_identifier = aws_rds_cluster.aurora.id
  instance_class     = "db.r6g.large"
  engine             = aws_rds_cluster.aurora.engine
  availability_zone  = ["us-east-1a","us-east-1b","us-east-1c"][count.index]
}
Deployments are a leading cause of availability incidents. Updating software on running instances introduces risk. Two strategies keep HA during deployments:
Update instances one at a time in the ASG; capacity temporarily reduced
Higher — partial rollout can cause mixed versions
Launch a new ASG (green), shift ALB traffic, terminate old ASG (blue)
Seconds — flip ALB back to blue
Route 5–10% to new version; monitor error rate; gradually shift to 100%
Database connections are a common HA bottleneck. Each EC2 instance in a large ASG opens its own connections. During a failover or burst, connection counts spike and can exhaust RDS max_connections:

— fully managed connection pool that sits between your application and RDS. Maintains a persistent pool of connections to RDS. During failover, RDS Proxy reconnects to the new primary transparently — application sees a brief pause, not a connection error.
— for PostgreSQL, PgBouncer on EC2 provides transaction-mode pooling. Lower overhead than RDS Proxy but you manage it.
for PostgreSQL: 10 GiB RAM = ~630 max connections. With 50 EC2 instances × 20 connections each = 1,000 connections. RDS Proxy is essential at this scale.
HA is not a one-time configuration — you must continuously verify that the system is operating in an available state. Key metrics to monitor:
ALB has fewer healthy targets than expected
One or more targets failing health checks
RDS Multi-AZ failover occurred — measure actual RTO
Read replica falling behind — stale reads
AWS's Well-Architected Framework defines the Reliability Pillar as the set of principles for ensuring a workload performs its intended function correctly and consistently. The core design principles:
— use health checks, ASG replacement, RDS Multi-AZ failover. Do not rely on manual intervention for common failure modes.
— regularly test failover, backup restoration, and deployment rollback. Use AWS FIS for failure injection.
— replace one large resource with many smaller ones. More instances = lower blast radius per failure.
— over-provisioning wastes money; under-provisioning causes availability incidents. Use Auto Scaling with target tracking.
— use Infrastructure as Code (Terraform, CDK) and CI/CD pipelines. Human error in manual changes is a leading cause of availability incidents.
Async Decoupling with SQS for HA

Synchronous communication between services couples their availability — if Service B is down, Service A fails too. Replacing synchronous calls with SQS queues decouples them: Service A always succeeds (messages go to the queue), and Service B processes them when available:
— at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering. Suitable for most HA use cases. Messages replicated across multiple AZs automatically.
— exactly-once delivery, strict ordering. Slightly lower throughput (3,000 msg/s with batching). Use when order matters (financial transactions, state machines).
— configure a DLQ to capture messages that fail processing after N retries. Without a DLQ, poison pill messages loop indefinitely and prevent the consumer from processing good messages.
— set longer than your maximum processing time. If a consumer crashes mid-processing, the message reappears for another consumer after the timeout.
Shared State: EFS vs S3 vs ElastiCache
Stateless EC2 instances improve HA, but some applications need shared state. Choose the right shared storage based on access patterns:
Stored across multiple AZs; mount targets per AZ
General purpose; burst or provisioned throughput

# EFS with access points for multi-AZ mount
resource "aws_efs_file_system" "shared" {
  creation_token   = "prod-shared-fs"
  performance_mode = "generalPurpose"
  throughput_mode  = "bursting"
  encrypted        = true
}

# One mount target per AZ
resource "aws_efs_mount_target" "mnt" {
  count           = 3
  file_system_id  = aws_efs_file_system.shared.id
  subnet_id       = aws_subnet.private[count.index].id
  security_groups = [aws_security_group.efs.id]
}
Auto Scaling Policies for HA Under Load
Auto Scaling is not just for cost optimization — it maintains availability under unexpected load spikes that would otherwise cause overload and cascading failures:
— set a target metric (e.g., ALB request count per target = 1,000). ASG adjusts instance count to maintain that target. Simplest and most reactive.
— define scaling steps triggered at metric thresholds (e.g., +2 instances at 70% CPU, +5 at 90% CPU). More control than target tracking.
— pre-scale for known load patterns (Monday morning spike, end-of-month batch). Avoids the lag of reactive scaling during predictable peaks.
— machine learning forecasts based on historical patterns; scales proactively before predicted load arrives. Works well for cyclic patterns.
Verifying HA: GameDays and Chaos Engineering
An HA architecture that has never experienced a real failure may have undetected gaps. Deliberate failure injection reveals them:
— a scheduled exercise where the team deliberately induces failures (terminate instances, block subnets, inject latency) and measures actual recovery time, alert quality, and runbook effectiveness.
— managed chaos engineering. Predefined actions: EC2 termination, RDS failover, ECS task stop, network disruption, CPU and memory stress. Experiment templates can be version-controlled.
— document the expected response to each failure scenario before GameDay. A RunbookDay (running through the runbook on a staging environment) is a useful intermediate step.
Use this checklist when designing or auditing an AWS architecture for high availability:
ASG min_size ≥ 2 (ideally 3), health_check_type = ELB, subnets span 3 AZs.
ALB deployed across ≥ 2 AZs, cross-zone load balancing enabled, deregistration delay configured.
RDS Multi-AZ enabled, or Aurora with ≥ 2 reader instances across AZs. Automated backups with adequate retention.
ElastiCache replication group with Multi-AZ and automatic failover enabled. Minimum 1 replica.
One NAT Gateway per AZ. Each private subnet routes to its AZ's NAT Gateway.
Route 53 for public DNS. Health checks on critical endpoints. Failover or latency routing policy configured.
SQS (not self-managed), with DLQ and visibility timeout > max processing time.
EBS for single-instance, EFS or S3 for multi-instance shared state.
Alarms on UnHealthyHostCount, RDS failover events, ASG instance health, queue depth.
Blue/green or canary — not in-place updates to all instances simultaneously.
At least one GameDay or FIS experiment run against production-equivalent environment.
High availability targets a specific uptime percentage (e.g., 99.99%) by automating recovery from failures, not by preventing all failures.
Composite availability multiplies each dependency's availability — a long chain of 99.9% services quickly degrades to 99% or worse. Minimize synchronous dependencies in the critical path.
The multi-AZ baseline: EC2 in ASG across 3 AZs, ALB with cross-zone load balancing, RDS Multi-AZ or Aurora, NAT Gateway per AZ, ElastiCache replication group with Multi-AZ.
Deployments are a top cause of availability incidents. Use blue/green or canary deployment strategies to shift traffic safely rather than updating in place.
Monitor HealthyHostCount, UnHealthyHostCount, RDS failover events, and connection counts — HA is an ongoing operational property, not a one-time architecture decision.
